Generally speaking, you will never have layoffs that are solely performance based. Doing so puts the company at risk, as it may get rid of positions which are actually needed. 

Usually, consulting companies on this area recommend using performance as a tie breaker or as an additional factor to consider; as opposed to being the main one.

messages.content: I have no real data to back my senses but the layoffs to shed overhiring in tech (massive salary cap) and poor financial ops management.  The slowing of the economy in terms of growth is very minimal, with bank rates still high but stop raising, and getting loans for big business is way harder than before with the crashing of lender early 2023… cash is king for business to extend their runway in hope the market truly recover back to growth/high risk investment. TLDR; besides large business cutting waste, some small business like startup will return back to their core  business (sustaining the same infrastructure) rather than expanding business. I surely hope I’m wrong.

The way I see it - a goal for a company is a responsible investment into R&D with proportional financial returns. In other words scale headcount so long as revenue per head is the same or increasing. If that ceases then you may have an efficiency issue or ineffective management. You may have a lot of cash in hand but it either goes back to shareholders (buybacks, dividends, etc)  or reinvests in the business in some form (R&D, M&A, Venture, etc). I feel like some of what we are seeing is resetting to take advantage of the situation so that greater efficiency is gained. And maybe to gain some resiliency and agility for the future. 

Not to say that this approach is desirable or right.  For TPM the challenge is how to articulate clear ROI and criticality. Ultimately every TPM hired is a trade off for ROI to someone. Even an engineer is a trade off on ROI for revenue per head. It would be interesting to hear a CFOs take.

On Blind there is this theory about a new tax rule where companies will now incur taxes on total cost of software development. Reducing headcount reduces taxes which is what triggered layoffs. Could be wrong but interesting theory.
does headcount towards the development budget?
Equity based comp puts a big forward liability on the balance sheet.  Layoffs take that right off the balance sheet as well as the annual loaded expense.

This is how it was at Google before there were thousands of TPMs (and Directors and VPs, and even Eng Managers). L6 TL/Ms ran their (relatively small) teams of highly proficient engineers. They required very limited oversight because each engineer was also very autonomous. What few TPMs there were focused on cross-company dependencies, deep/niche engineering problems, or external logistics (dealing with partner companies etc). This all worked well until growth really picked up (60k+ engineers). It still works well in teams that had sustainable growth over the decade.

Reminds me a little of how the SDET role ended up being almost absorbed or phased into SWE over time. There are still some pockets that have dedicated SDETs but major change from before. 

The TPM role is also fairly new in the last 10 years or so. There's such a broad slice of the TPM role in tech all the way from PgM -> TPM -> TPdM -> PdM -> PMM so maybe it'll be a redistribution going forward?

Latest email from The Information says that stock based comp dilution may be a big reason. Net loss of $1.1b of which $1b was stock based comp. 

"first nine months of last year, Snap reported an operating loss of $1.1 billion after taking into account roughly $1 billion in stock comp expense. In other words, without stock comp, Snap’s operating loss would have been around $100 million. It’s to Spiegel’s credit that he isn’t just trying to tell investors to look the other way. Then again, long-term investors, who are most hurt by the share count dilution that results from stock compensation, have probably made their dislike of stock comp clear to him. There have already been signs he was taking notice of their concerns. Last quarter, Snap announced a stock buyback program, which it said was intended to offset the dilution caused by stock-based compensation. It noted then that “we view careful management of our share count as a critical input to long-term shareholder returns.” 

Snap’s stock price, then trading below $10, has rallied a lot since then, but lately it has been stuck just under $17. Given that Snap went public in 2017 at $17 a share, you can imagine Spiegel wants to get it above that level. Unfortunately for him, investor reaction to the layoff news today wasn’t positive: Snap shares fell nearly 2%. But that might be because the company is reporting fourth-quarter earnings on Tuesday, and investors may see the layoff news as a sign that the company will report a weak fourth-quarter result. To be sure, Snap can only win over investors completely by showing it has a growth path ahead. But all things being equal, reducing the dilution from stock compensation can’t hurt."

If a company is not getting steady stock investment and appreciation, stock based comp will eventually collapse.  Getting rid of employees is the quickest way to eliminate the rapidly accumulating liability.

messages.content:
IMHO, Layoffs are happening especially in tech because most companies overhired and are overvalued due to the influx of investment driven by ZIRP and dovish economic policy during the pandemic. What we're seeing is a return to the mean. 

AI is just current scapegoat for economic woes. The curious dichotomy is that it's difficult not to characterize what's happening with AI as a bubble given the capital investment vs. ROI. 

My take is we're seeing an increase in vibe code in production, followed by an increase in security incidents. I believe the big tech companies know this; google is buying wiz / servicenow buying armis.
Am I the only one noticing major Apps appear to be vibe-coded fragile garbage? E.g. Redfin is....
From my (relaitvely little) experience on it all, I don't see how vibe coding replaces developers.  I do think it should make Product and Program people more efficient (and so be asked to do more).

I’m a product manager and this quarter our entire company leadership has started to go all in on AI, there is a new gate on all PRs for it to be co-authored by Claude. All goals and acceptance criteria needs to be  reviewed or generated with Claude. Instead of stories we are now handing entire features off to a single dev with some high level goals and letting the engineer work with Claude to build a dev / implementation plan. Our leadership perceives the cost of re-work as much lower with Claude now so in their eyes the old approach which was more heavy on planning, refinement, etc. will slow us down too much. It’s going to be interesting because we are in a very sensitive industry (cybersecurity saas), I don’t think our customers have the same appetite for re-work as we do internally.

I’m not sure if this paradigm will continue, maybe we will become an AI software factory, maybe we will crash and burn. But if this continues I think we will need much less non senior devs / architects.
Thats really intense, ive mostly been seeing leaning into this philosophy but being cautious
